mappings.rationaleAI: South Africa's history from Khoisan and Bantu-speaking peoples through Dutch and British colonization, the Mineral Revolution, and Anglo-Boer tensions provides broad coverage across southern African geography (A), pre-1500 developments (B), and post-1500 developments including Dutch settlement and mineral discoveries (C).

African history's coverage of the Bantu expansion and European colonial penetration provides context across southern Africa's geography (A), pre-1500 migrations (B), and colonial-era developments (C).
Stone Age developments relate to the origins of mankind and development of culture in the Stone Age in southern Africa (B.1).

The Iron Age in sub-Saharan Africa relates to Iron Age development and Bantu-speaking peoples' migrations (B.1) and the Late Iron Age southeast coast trade and interior routes to Great Zimbabwe (B.2).
The slave trade relates to Portuguese control of the slave trade in west central Africa (C.2) and the slave and ivory trade north of the Zambezi (C.5).
Portuguese exploration relates to Portuguese expansion into the Zambezi valley (C.1).

The British Empire's expansion into southern Africa relates to Boer and Xhosa resistance to the British (C.6), establishment of Natal and Cape Colony (C.6.b), and the era of mineral discoveries (C.7).
The Scramble for Africa relates to the era of mineral discoveries, confederation, and European competition for southern African territories (C.7).
The DRC's Congo Basin location and history under Leopold II provide context for Portuguese and later European activities in west central Africa (C.2).
The Silk Road's maritime routes connecting East Africa relate to the southeast coast trade in the Late Iron Age and interior trade routes (B.2).
Swahili as a Bantu language of East Africa relates to the Bantu-speaking peoples and the southeast coast trade network (B.2).
Africa's overview including the Bantu expansion and European colonialism provides broad context for the geography and achievements of southern African civilizations (A).
The post-classical period's geographic expansion of civilizations provides context for southern Africa before c. 1500 (B).
The early modern period's European colonization provides context for southern Africa from c. 1500 to c. 1885 (C).
